title: Defining and Calling Functions
subtitle: Learn how to create reusable blocks of code with the def keyword
learning_objectives: Use the def keyword to define a function
Understand the role of parentheses and the colon in a function definition
Write a properly indented function body
Understand that functions only run when called
Explain why functions help avoid repetition and organize code

sections.heading: What Is a Function?
sections.content:
A function is a named, reusable block of code. You define it once and can run it as many times as you like. This avoids copying the same code in multiple places and makes programs easier to read and maintain.

To define a function in Python you use the def keyword, followed by the function name, a pair of parentheses (), and a colon :. The body of the function must be indented — Python uses indentation to know which lines belong to the function.
sections.code_example:
def greet():
    print("Hello, World!")

# The function is now defined but has NOT run yet.
# Call it by using its name followed by ():
greet()

sections.heading: Functions Run Only When Called
sections.content: Defining a function with def does not execute its body. The code inside only runs when you call the function by writing its name with parentheses. You can call the same function many times, which is one of the main benefits — write the logic once, use it everywhere.
sections.code_example:
def say_goodbye():
    print("Goodbye!")

# Nothing printed yet — function is defined but not called.

say_goodbye()   # First call
say_goodbye()   # Second call

exercises.title: Define and Call greet()
exercises.prompt: Define a function called greet that prints Hello. Then call it.
exercises.starter_code:
# Define a function called greet that prints Hello
# Then call the function
hints: Indent the print statement inside the function
After the function definition, call it with greet()

exercises.title: Define and Call welcome()
exercises.prompt: Define a function called welcome that prints Welcome to Python!. Call it twice to see the benefit of reuse.
exercises.starter_code:
# Define a function called welcome
# It should print: Welcome to Python!
# Call it twice
exercises.solution:
def welcome():
    print("Welcome to Python!")

welcome()
welcome()
hints: Use def welcome(): to start the function
Put print("Welcome to Python!") inside, indented
Write welcome() twice after the definition to call it two times

quiz.question: Which of the following correctly starts a Python function definition?
quiz.explanation: Python uses the def keyword, followed by the function name, parentheses, and a colon. The other options use keywords from different languages.

quiz.question: When does the body of a function execute?
options: Only when the function is called
As soon as Python reads the def statement
Only once, when the script starts
Every time Python encounters a print statement
quiz.explanation: Defining a function with def simply stores the code for later. The body runs only when you explicitly call the function using its name followed by ().

completion_message: Great work! You have defined and called your first Python functions. Remember: def creates the function, but parentheses () are what make it run.
